bibliographic_data.title: Lattice dynamics in mono- and few-layer sheets of WS2 and WSe2
bibliographic_data.abstract: Thickness is one of the fundamental parameters that define the electronic, optical, and thermal properties of two-dimensional (2D) crystals. Phonons in molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) were recently found to exhibit unique thickness dependence due to interplay between short and long range interactions. Here we report Raman spectra of atomically thin sheets of WS2 and WSe2, isoelectronic compounds of MoS2, in the mono- to few-layer thickness regime. We show that, similar to the case of MoS2, the characteristic 1g A and 12g E modes exhibit stiffening and softening with increasing number of layers, respectively, with a small shift of less than 3 cm-1 due to large mass of the atoms. Thickness dependence is also observed in a series of multiphonon bands arising from overtone, combination, and zone edge phonons, whose intensity exhibit significant enhancement in excitonic resonance conditions. Some of these multiphonon peaks are found to be absent only in monolayers. These features provide a unique fingerprint and rapid identification for monolayer flakes.

core_content.novelty_claim: The study reports the thickness dependence of Raman modes in WS2 and WSe2, isoelectronic compounds of MoS2, and finds unique phonon behaviors that provide a fingerprint for monolayer flakes.
core_content.key_methodology: Raman spectroscopy was performed on mono- to few-layer sheets of WS2 and WSe2 using three excitation wavelengths (472, 532, and 633 nm) to investigate the effects of excitonic resonance conditions on multiphonon bands.
outcomes: The characteristic 1g A mode stiffens with increasing number of layers while the 12g E mode softens.
Multiphonon bands exhibit significant intensity enhancement in excitonic resonance conditions.
Some multiphonon peaks are absent only in monolayers.
core_content.full_text_markdown: This study investigates the lattice dynamics of mono- and few-layer sheets of WS2 and WSe2, isoelectronic compounds of MoS2. Using Raman spectroscopy with three excitation wavelengths (472, 532, and 633 nm), the researchers observed that the characteristic 1g A mode stiffens while the 12g E mode softens with increasing number of layers, similar to the behavior seen in MoS2. Additionally, multiphonon bands exhibit significant intensity enhancement under excitonic resonance conditions, and some peaks are absent only in monolayers. These findings provide unique fingerprints for identifying monolayer flakes.
